narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model, trained on data from thousands of past students, estimated a 58% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try—above the threshold we use to ensure strong outcomes for admitted students. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, a Year 3 decile of 4, low family income, an LSAT score of 20, an undergraduate GPA of 2.8, identifying as female and Asian, and planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who average higher deciles around 5.7 for Year 1 and 5.5 for Year 3, LSAT scores near 37, GPAs around 3.2, and mostly White full-time backgrounds—your metrics fell below these benchmarks across the board. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward failure since it's lower than average. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also exerting a strong pull in the same direction due to being below the norm. Low family income ranked next in influence, further tipping the scales toward a fail prediction. Your LSAT score followed closely, with its low value compared to peers contributing significantly to the risk assessment. Being Asian, a less common background in the dataset dominated by White students, played a similar role in heightening the predicted risk. Your undergraduate GPA came next, reinforcing the concern with its below-average mark. Factors like gender and full-time status had the least sway but still nudged slightly toward failure. Overall, these elements together painted a picture riskier than the successful profiles the model favors. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim higher than 37, as scores are changeable with preparation; build stronger academic habits to boost future grades or equivalents; and explore income-support resources like scholarships to offset family challenges. With targeted effort on these realistic areas, you could shift your profile closer to top performers and strengthen future applications. Keep pushing—you have the determination to turn this around.
